narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 52.8% chance that you would not pass—crossing the threshold we use to recommend rejection. This prediction draws from your academic record, including your undergraduate GPA of 3.10 and LSAT score of 31.50, alongside your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings, family income in the middle bracket, and your plan to attend full-time. Among students who passed the bar, averages are higher, like LSAT scores around 37 and undergraduate GPAs near 3.24, with most coming from upper-middle or high-income backgrounds and ranking in the top half of their classes. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 decile ranking of 2, which stood out as a major factor pushing the prediction toward failure since it placed you well below the typical 6th decile for passers. Next in influence was your undergraduate GPA, which actually worked in your favor by nudging the odds slightly toward passing, as it was reasonably competitive compared to successful students. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed toward the failure prediction, reinforcing concerns about early academic performance relative to the 6th decile average for those who passed. While your full-time status aligns with the vast majority of bar passers and your middle-income background is common enough, these didn't outweigh the key academic signals. This close call at 52.8% shows you're not far off, especially with your GPA providing some positive lift. To strengthen a future application, focus on boosting your academic deciles through targeted study habits or additional coursework, perhaps retaking the LSAT to aim closer to 37 or higher. Consider gaining practical experience, like internships, to build a stronger profile. Many students in similar positions have succeeded by addressing these gaps—reapplying after clear improvements could shift the prediction in your favor. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
